bite.definition: Here used in the phrase 'come back to bite' meaning to cause problems later.
bite.example: Ignoring your problems will come back to bite you.

legacy.definition: Something passed down from the past; what was left to you by earlier generations.
legacy.example: Our parents leave us a legacy of habits and values.

cliche.definition: A phrase or idea that has been used so often it is no longer interesting or original.
cliche.example: Saying 'time heals all wounds' is a cliche.

behaviour.definition: The way a person acts or conducts themselves.
behaviour.example: Her behaviour changed when she started school.

consider.example: Consider how your words affect your child.

essential.example: Sleep is essential for good health.

role.definition: The part someone plays in a situation or in another person's life.
role.example: Parents play a key role in their child's development.

models.definition: People whose behaviour is copied by others, especially children.
models.example: Parents are the first role models for children.

influence.definition: The power to change how someone thinks, feels, or acts.
influence.example: Parents have a strong influence on their children.

trigger.definition: To cause a strong emotional reaction, often from a past experience.
trigger.example: Loud noises can trigger fear in some people.

mistakenly.example: She mistakenly thought he was angry at her.

dealings.definition: The way you interact with or treat someone.
dealings.example: Be fair in your dealings with children.

critic.definition: A voice (real or in your head) that finds fault and judges harshly.
critic.example: Everyone has an inner critic that tells them they are not good enough.

inner.definition: Inside your mind; not spoken out loud.
inner.example: Your inner voice can be your biggest enemy.

damaging.example: Harsh words can be damaging to a child.

generation.definition: All the people born and living around the same time; parents and children are different generations.

warmth.definition: A friendly and caring feeling toward someone.
warmth.example: Children need warmth and love from their parents.

boundaries.definition: Rules or limits that show what is acceptable behavior.
boundaries.example: Children need love and clear boundaries.

soothing.definition: Making someone feel calm and less upset.
soothing.example: A soothing voice can help a scared child.

circumstances.definition: The conditions and facts around a situation.

childcare.definition: The care and supervision of children, especially by someone other than the parents.
childcare.example: Good childcare is hard to find.

busyness.definition: The state of being very busy and having many things to do.
busyness.example: Busyness can stop parents from spending time with their children.

exhaustive.definition: Very thorough and complete, covering every detail.
exhaustive.example: This is not an exhaustive list of problems.

confidence.definition: The feeling of believing in yourself and your abilities.

pessimism.definition: The habit of expecting bad things to happen.
pessimism.example: His pessimism made it hard for him to enjoy life.

defences.definition: Ways we protect ourselves from feeling painful emotions.
defences.example: He built defences to avoid feeling hurt.

overwhelmed.definition: Feeling like there is too much to handle; completely overcome.
overwhelmed.example: She felt overwhelmed by all her responsibilities.

irritates.definition: Makes someone slightly angry or annoyed.
irritates.example: It irritates me when people are late.

expectations.definition: Beliefs about what should happen or what someone should be like.
expectations.example: Parents often have high expectations for their children.

millennia.example: Humans have been raising children for millennia.

chain.definition: A series of connected things, one after another.
chain.example: Each generation is a link in a chain.

reshape.definition: To change the form or character of something.
reshape.example: You can reshape your habits with practice.

ditch.definition: To get rid of something you no longer want.
ditch.example: It is time to ditch old bad habits.

unpack.definition: To examine something carefully by looking at all its parts.
unpack.example: Let's unpack what happened during your childhood.

familiar.definition: Well known to you because you have seen or experienced it before.

examine.definition: To look at something carefully and in detail.
examine.example: Take time to examine your own behavior.

respected.definition: Treated with care and consideration; valued.
respected.example: Every child deserves to be respected.

unconditional.definition: Without any conditions or limits; complete and total.
unconditional.example: Parents should give their children unconditional love.

rewarding.definition: Giving you satisfaction and a sense of achievement.
rewarding.example: Having close family relationships is very rewarding.

blueprint.definition: A detailed plan or guide for how to build or do something.
blueprint.example: A happy childhood gives you a blueprint for good relationships.

functional.example: They built a functional and loving family.

contribute.definition: To give something (effort, time, ideas) to help achieve a result.
contribute.example: Everyone can contribute to the community.

proportion.definition: A part or share of a whole group.
proportion.example: A large proportion of people struggle with this.

discomfort.definition: A feeling of being slightly upset, uneasy, or in pain.
discomfort.example: Looking at your past may bring some discomfort.

self-aware.definition: Knowing your own feelings, character, and behaviour.
self-aware.example: Being self-aware helps you become a better parent.

mindful.definition: Paying careful attention to your thoughts and feelings in the moment.
mindful.example: Be mindful of how your words affect your child.

inherited.definition: Received from parents or earlier generations through behavior or genes.
inherited.example: Many of our habits are inherited from our parents.

awareness.definition: Knowledge and understanding of what is happening.
awareness.example: Self-awareness is the first step to change.

rooted.definition: Coming from or based in something deep.
rooted.example: His anger was rooted in his childhood.

illustrate.definition: To show or explain something by giving an example.
illustrate.example: This story will illustrate my point.

psychotherapist.definition: A person trained to help people with emotional problems by talking with them.
psychotherapist.example: A psychotherapist can help you understand your feelings.

mentioning.example: I'm mentioning this because it is important.

well-meaning.definition: Having good intentions, even if the result is not perfect.
well-meaning.example: She was well-meaning but her advice was wrong.

slip.definition: To accidentally fall into an old habit or pattern.
slip.example: Even experts slip into old patterns sometimes.

warp.definition: A period where you are mentally transported to another time.
warp.example: Old feelings can create an emotional time warp.

climbing.definition: The activity of going up something using your hands and feet.
climbing.example: The children played on the climbing frame.

frame.definition: A structure made of bars or poles for climbing or supporting something.
frame.example: She got stuck on the climbing frame.

furious.example: He was furious when he found out the truth.

ridiculous.example: The idea seemed ridiculous at first.

howled.definition: Cried very loudly with a long sound.
howled.example: The child howled when she fell down.

guilt.definition: A feeling of having done something wrong.
guilt.example: He felt a flash of guilt about his behavior.

fearfully.definition: In a way that shows fear or worry.
fearfully.example: The child looked up fearfully at her parent.

encouraging.example: Her encouraging words helped him try again.

grab.definition: To take hold of something quickly and firmly.
grab.example: Grab the bar and pull yourself up.

princess.definition: The daughter of a king or queen; here means treated as someone very delicate.
princess.example: Her grandmother treated her like a princess.

incapable.example: Being overprotected can make a child feel incapable.

overcome.definition: To be affected so strongly by a feeling that you cannot think clearly.

judgements.definition: Opinions or decisions formed quickly, sometimes without enough thought.
judgements.example: Be careful about making quick judgements.

assumptions.definition: Things you believe to be true without proof.
assumptions.example: Don't make assumptions about why your child is upset.

reaction.definition: A response to something that happens.

triggered.definition: Caused to happen; set off an emotional response.
triggered.example: The situation triggered memories from his past.

resentment.definition: A feeling of anger about something unfair that happened to you.
resentment.example: He carried resentment from his childhood.

envy.definition: A feeling of wanting what someone else has.
envy.example: She felt envy when she saw her friend's success.

disgust.definition: A strong feeling of dislike or disapproval.
disgust.example: He looked at the mess with disgust.

panic.definition: A sudden strong feeling of fear that makes it hard to think clearly.
panic.example: She felt panic when she lost her child in the crowd.

irritation.definition: A slight feeling of anger or annoyance.
irritation.example: Her irritation grew as the noise continued.

dread.definition: A strong feeling of fear about something that might happen.
dread.example: She felt dread every Sunday night before work.

et cetera.definition: And other similar things (Latin phrase often written as 'etc.').
et cetera.example: We bought bread, milk, eggs, et cetera.

warning.definition: A sign that something bad might happen or that you should be careful.
warning.example: Take your child's anger as a warning sign.

buttons.definition: Here means sensitive emotional spots that cause strong reactions when touched.
buttons.example: Your children know how to press your buttons.

pressed.definition: Pushed; here means your emotional weak spots have been activated.
pressed.example: When your buttons are pressed, stop and think.

pattern.definition: A regular and repeated way of behaving.
pattern.example: There is a pattern to how she reacts.

charged.example: His voice was charged with anger.

defend.definition: To protect yourself from something threatening.
defend.example: We defend ourselves from painful feelings.

despair.example: She felt despair when nothing seemed to work.

longing.definition: A deep, strong feeling of wanting something you cannot have.
longing.example: He felt a longing for his childhood home.

loneliness.definition: The sad feeling of being alone and having no one close to you.
loneliness.example: Loneliness can affect people at any age.

jealousy.definition: An unhappy feeling of wanting what someone else has.

neediness.definition: The quality of needing too much attention or emotional support from others.

empathizing.definition: Understanding and sharing the feelings of another person.
empathizing.example: Empathizing with your child helps them feel safe.

short-circuit.definition: To skip the normal process and jump straight to a reaction.
short-circuit.example: Instead of thinking, he short-circuits to anger.

shrieks.definition: Very high, loud screams or cries.
shrieks.example: The shrieks of children playing filled the park.

alert.definition: A state of being very watchful and ready to react.
alert.example: She went into an alert state when she heard the noise.

appropriately.definition: In a way that is suitable and correct for the situation.

noisily.definition: In a loud way that makes a lot of noise.
noisily.example: The children played noisily in the yard.

tiptoe.definition: To walk very quietly on the front part of your feet.
tiptoe.example: The children had to tiptoe so they wouldn't disturb their grandfather.

scared.example: She was scared to admit her true feelings.

admit.definition: To say or accept that something is true, often something you don't want to accept.
admit.example: It is hard to admit you were wrong.

intensify.definition: To become or make something stronger or more extreme.
intensify.example: Ignoring a problem can intensify it.

naming.definition: Saying what something is; identifying and putting words to something.
naming.example: Naming your feelings helps you deal with them.

inconvenient.definition: Causing trouble or difficulty; not suitable.
inconvenient.example: These inconvenient feelings are still important.

narrative.definition: A story or explanation that gives meaning to events.
narrative.example: She found a new narrative for her anger.

responsible.definition: Being the cause of something; having the duty to take care of something.
responsible.example: Children are not responsible for their parents' feelings.

fault.definition: The reason for a mistake or problem; blame.
fault.example: It is not the child's fault.

trace.definition: To follow something back to its beginning or origin.
trace.example: Can you trace this feeling back to your childhood?

annoying.definition: Making someone slightly angry or bothered.

exasperating.example: Dealing with tantrums can be exasperating.

clumsy.definition: Moving in an awkward way; tending to drop things or fall.
clumsy.example: The clumsy child kept dropping his cup.

default.definition: The usual or automatic way of doing something when you don't make a different choice.
default.example: Yelling is his default response to stress.

overnight.definition: Very suddenly, in a very short time.
overnight.example: Overnight, your child becomes your biggest priority.

demanding.definition: Requiring a lot of attention, time, or effort.
demanding.example: Having a baby is very demanding.

priority.definition: Something that is more important than other things and should be done first.
priority.example: Your child should be your top priority.

realize.definition: To suddenly understand something you did not know before.
realize.example: She didn't realize how tired she was.

appreciate.definition: To understand and be thankful for something.
appreciate.example: I appreciate everything my parents did for me.

identify.definition: To recognize or understand something; to feel connected to someone's experience.
identify.example: Parents often identify with their own parents.

compassion.definition: A deep feeling of care and concern for someone who is suffering.
compassion.example: Show compassion to yourself and your child.

contemplating.definition: Thinking deeply and carefully about something.
contemplating.example: Spend time contemplating what your child might be feeling.

empathy.definition: The ability to understand and share another person's feelings.
empathy.example: Empathy helps you connect with your child.

client.definition: A person who receives professional help or services.
client.example: The therapist met with her client every week.

adopted.definition: Legally took another person's child to raise as your own.
adopted.example: They adopted a baby boy last year.

rage.example: He felt rage rise up when his child misbehaved.

rapping.example: His grandfather punished him by rapping his knuckles.

knuckles.definition: The hard bumps on your fingers where they bend.
knuckles.example: He rapped the child's knuckles with a knife handle.

patience.definition: The ability to wait calmly and accept delays without getting angry.
patience.example: Parenting requires a lot of patience.

assume.definition: To believe something is true without proof.
assume.example: Don't assume you know what your child is feeling.

recipient.example: The child was the happy recipient of many gifts.

unconsciously.definition: Without being aware of it; without realizing.
unconsciously.example: We unconsciously repeat our parents' habits.

undeserving.example: No child is undeserving of love.

leaks.definition: Comes out slowly and often without meaning to.
leaks.example: Her frustration leaks out in a sharp tone.

unreasonably.definition: In a way that is not fair or sensible.
unreasonably.example: He was unreasonably expecting a toddler to share.

mature.definition: Behaving in a grown-up, sensible way.
mature.example: A four-year-old cannot be expected to be mature.

competitive.definition: Wanting to win or do better than others.
competitive.example: He felt competitive with his own child.

lash.definition: To suddenly attack or speak angrily.
lash.example: He would lash out at his child in anger.

anonymous.definition: Done by someone whose name is not known.
anonymous.example: The angry comments came from anonymous sources.

jealous.definition: Feeling unhappy because someone has something you want.
jealous.example: Some parents feel jealous of their own children.

trolling.definition: Saying hurtful things to someone, usually online, to upset them.

exercises.definition: Activities designed to help you practice or learn something.
exercises.example: The book includes exercises to help you understand yourself.

overwhelming.definition: So strong or powerful that it is hard to deal with.
overwhelming.example: The feelings were overwhelming at first.

skip.definition: To pass over something; to not do it.
skip.example: You can skip the exercise and come back to it later.

wholly.example: Does this feeling wholly belong to the present?

standpoint.definition: The way someone sees things; their point of view.
standpoint.example: Try to see the situation from their standpoint.

calm.definition: To become or make someone quiet and peaceful.
calm.example: Take a moment to calm down before speaking.

guidance.definition: Help and advice about how to do something or how to behave.
guidance.example: Children need guidance from their parents.

variation.definition: A slightly different form of something.
variation.example: Try this variation of the exercise.

self-righteous.definition: Feeling certain that you are morally right and others are wrong.
self-righteous.example: Don't be self-righteous about your parenting style.

indignant.definition: Feeling angry because something seems unfair or wrong.
indignant.example: She was indignant when accused unfairly.

panicky.definition: Feeling sudden strong fear that makes it hard to think.
panicky.example: She felt panicky when the child ran toward the road.

ashamed.definition: Feeling embarrassed or guilty about something you did or something about yourself.
ashamed.example: He felt ashamed of how he treated his child.

self-loathing.definition: A strong feeling of hatred toward yourself.
self-loathing.example: Self-loathing can prevent people from being good parents.

disconnected.definition: Not feeling emotionally close to others; separate.
disconnected.example: He felt disconnected from his family.

tracing.definition: Following something back to find where it started.
tracing.example: Tracing your feelings back to childhood can be helpful.

habit.definition: Something you do regularly, often without thinking about it.
habit.example: Bad habits are hard to break.

spoilt.definition: Given too many things or too much attention, making a child expect everything.
spoilt.example: They called the child spoilt for not sharing.

sharply.definition: In a harsh, sudden, or angry way.
sharply.example: She spoke sharply to the child.

presents.definition: Gifts given to someone, especially on a special occasion.
presents.example: The child received a pile of presents on her birthday.

pile.definition: A large amount of things placed on top of each other.
pile.example: There was a huge pile of presents.

birthday.definition: The day each year that is the same date as when a person was born.
birthday.example: They had a party for her birthday.
